title: Dog Head Pressing Against Wall: Signs, Causes, and Emergency Care
meta_desc: If your dog is pressing their head against a wall, it is a medical emergency. Learn why this happens and why you must see a veterinarian immediately.
content_html:
What is Head Pressing in Dogs?

Head pressing is a compulsive behavior where a dog stands close to a wall, corner, or other hard object and firmly presses the top of their head against it. Unlike seeking attention or a "head-butt" for affection, this behavior is usually persistent and the dog may seem stuck or distressed. It is often a sign of serious neurological impairment or damage to the forebrain.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

Yes. The urgency level for head pressing is High. This is considered a critical medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary intervention. It often indicates severe underlying issues such as brain trauma, toxic poisoning, liver failure (hepatic encephalopathy), or a brain tumor. Do not wait for symptoms to resolve on their own; contact an emergency clinic right away.

Why a Photo or Video Helps with Triage

When you call or arrive at the vet, having a short video or photo of the behavior is incredibly valuable. Neurological symptoms can sometimes be intermittent or change by the time you reach the clinic. Visual evidence allows the veterinarian to see the exact nature of the behavior, its intensity, and any accompanying signs like dilated pupils or aimless pacing, ensuring a faster and more accurate triage process.

faqs.question: What are the common causes of head pressing?
faqs.answer: Common causes include metabolic disorders like liver shunts, lead poisoning, brain tumors, strokes, or inflammatory diseases like encephalitis.

faqs.question: Is head pressing different from head rubbing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Head rubbing is often associated with skin allergies or ear infections. Head pressing involves sustained, firm pressure against a stationary object and is a neurological sign.

faqs.question: What other symptoms might I see?
faqs.answer: You may also notice aimless circling, changes in vision, seizures, or a sudden change in temperament or responsiveness.
